history: Child presenting with acute breathlessness, wheezing, cough for 2 days. Known asthmatic since age 3. Poorly controlled on salbutamol inhaler.
progression: Deteriorating over 48 hours despite home nebulization. Parents report night-time symptoms worsening.

clinical_timeline.event: Emergency presentation with acute wheeze and SpO2 88%
IV Hydrocortisone + back-to-back nebulization initiated
SpO2 improved to 95%, wheeze reduced
Oral prednisolone started, step-up inhaler therapy planned
Discharge with LABA/ICS combination + follow-up in 1 week

best_treatment: IV Hydrocortisone + Nebulized Ipratropium/Salbutamol, step-up to ICS/LABA on discharge
secondary_treatment: Oral Montelukast, allergen avoidance counseling, asthma action plan
risk_reasoning: Pediatric patient with SpO2 88% on arrival, no response to home nebulization, known poorly-controlled asthma — high short-term deterioration risk.
ai_rationale: Acute exacerbation severity indicated by oxygen desaturation and refractory bronchospasm. Pediatric age group elevates risk classification. Insurance cover supports HDU admission.

next_action.action: Arrange Paediatric HDU bed — Emergency admission

history: 55-year-old female with 3-hour history of chest pain radiating to left arm, associated diaphoresis. Known hypertensive, diabetic Type 2 for 10 years.
progression: Pain started at rest, not relieved by antacids. Brought to ER by family. BP 160/95 on arrival.

clinical_timeline.event: ER admission, ECG shows ST depression, aspirin 325mg + GTN given
Troponin I: 0.06 ng/mL (borderline), serial ECG ordered
Cardiology review — NSTEMI ruled in, heparin infusion started
Coronary angiography performed — 70% LAD stenosis found
PCI with stent placement, patient stable post-procedure
Discharge on dual antiplatelet therapy + statin

best_treatment: Urgent PCI, dual antiplatelet (Aspirin + Clopidogrel), heparin, high-intensity statin
secondary_treatment: Beta-blocker, ACE inhibitor, glycemic control, cardiac rehab referral
risk_reasoning: Active chest pain at rest with ECG changes and borderline troponin in a diabetic hypertensive female. High TIMI score. Risk of STEMI conversion within hours.
ai_rationale: ST depression with ongoing rest pain and multiple cardiac risk factors classify this as ACS requiring immediate invasive strategy. Cash patient — high revenue potential from PCI pathway.

next_action.action: Admit to ICU/CCU immediately

history: 72-year-old male, fell in bathroom 6 hours ago. Unable to bear weight on right leg. Known osteoporosis, on calcium supplements. History of hypertension.
red_flags: Age >70 with hip fracture — 30-day mortality risk 10%
progression: Fall from standing height. X-ray confirms intertrochanteric fracture. Pre-op workup initiated.

clinical_timeline.event: ER admission, X-ray confirms fracture, analgesia + skin traction applied
Pre-op workup: ECG, Echo, blood work — cleared for surgery
PFNA (Proximal Femoral Nail Anti-rotation) surgery performed
Physio mobilization started — partial weight bearing
Delirium episode managed, anti-osteoporosis treatment started
Discharge to step-down care with walker, family education done

best_treatment: PFNA fixation within 48 hours, early mobilization, VTE prophylaxis
secondary_treatment: Bisphosphonate therapy, fall prevention program, delirium prevention protocol
risk_reasoning: Elderly patient with hip fracture — high 30-day mortality risk. Cognitive impairment increases post-op delirium risk. Delay to surgery beyond 48h worsens outcomes.
ai_rationale: Hip fracture in elderly with osteoporosis requires urgent surgical fixation. Multiple comorbidities and age elevate risk score to 8. Insurance cover enables full surgical pathway.

next_action.action: Arrange Orthopaedic Ward bed — Emergency admission

history: 28-year-old primigravida at 34 weeks gestation presenting with headache, visual disturbances, BP 160/110. Pedal oedema for 1 week. Urine dipstick 3+ protein.
progression: BP progressively elevated over 2 weeks. Now with visual symptoms and proteinuria suggesting end-organ involvement.

clinical_timeline.event: Emergency admission, IV Labetalol + MgSO4 prophylaxis started
Fetal monitoring: CTG reactive, BPP 8/8
BP partially controlled, steroids given for fetal lung maturity
Platelets dropped to 82,000 — HELLP syndrome confirmed
Emergency LSCS performed at 34+2 weeks, baby 1.9kg, NICU admitted
Mother stabilized, antihypertensives continued post-partum
Discharge with BP monitoring plan, NICU baby on follow-up

best_treatment: IV Labetalol for BP control, MgSO4 seizure prophylaxis, emergency LSCS if HELLP confirmed
secondary_treatment: Corticosteroids for fetal lung maturity, post-partum antihypertensives
risk_reasoning: Severe preeclampsia with HELLP syndrome development. Both maternal and fetal lives at risk. Requires emergency delivery within 24-48 hours.
ai_rationale: Severe preeclampsia criteria met with end-organ involvement (HELLP). Risk score 9 reflects dual risk to mother and fetus. Emergency surgical delivery pathway indicated.

next_action.action: Admit to ICU/CCU immediately

primary_diagnosis: Acute Ischaemic Stroke — Left MCA Territory
history: 63-year-old male with sudden onset right-sided weakness and aphasia for 2 hours. Known atrial fibrillation, not on anticoagulation. Smoker, hypertensive.
red_flags: Symptom onset < 4.5 hours — thrombolysis window open
progression: Sudden onset 2 hours ago at home. FAST positive. Brought to ER within 45 minutes. Non-contrast CT: no haemorrhage.

clinical_timeline.event: ER arrival, NIHSS 14, CT head — no bleed, thrombolysis given within 3.5hrs
MRI brain confirms left MCA territory infarct
Partial neurological improvement — NIHSS improved to 10
Echo shows LAA thrombus — anticoagulation started cautiously after 48h
Swallow assessment passed, oral anticoagulant started
Transfer to stroke rehab unit for physiotherapy and speech therapy

best_treatment: IV tPA thrombolysis within window, anticoagulation for AF, aspirin + statin
secondary_treatment: Physiotherapy, speech therapy, dysphagia management, blood pressure control
risk_reasoning: Acute ischaemic stroke with moderate-severe deficit (NIHSS 14) in thrombolysis window. Cardioembolic etiology from untreated AF. High disability and mortality risk without immediate intervention.
ai_rationale: Time-critical intervention window open. AF without anticoagulation is a modifiable risk factor that caused this event. NIHSS 14 indicates significant disability risk requiring stroke unit care.

next_action.action: Admit to ICU/CCU immediately

history: 42-year-old female with Type 1 DM presenting with vomiting, abdominal pain, altered sensorium for 1 day. Blood glucose 480 mg/dL, ketones 4+, pH 7.18.
progression: Missed insulin doses for 2 days due to vomiting illness. Progressively worsening over 24 hours.

clinical_timeline.event: ER admission, IV fluids 1L NS bolus, insulin infusion started
Potassium 3.1 — IV KCl replacement started
pH improved to 7.28, glucose 220, ketones 2+
Anion gap closing, transitioned to SC insulin regimen
Eating normally, ketones cleared, discharged with insulin counseling

best_treatment: IV insulin infusion, aggressive IV fluid replacement, potassium supplementation, hourly monitoring
secondary_treatment: Identify precipitating cause, diabetes education, sick-day rules counseling
risk_reasoning: Severe DKA with pH 7.18 and altered sensorium. Risk of cerebral oedema, hypokalaemia-induced arrhythmia, and aspiration. Requires ICU-level monitoring.
ai_rationale: Severe metabolic acidosis with altered consciousness classifies as high acuity DKA. Type 1 DM with compliance issues elevates recurrence risk. Insurance covers full DKA management pathway.

next_action.action: Arrange ICU / HDU bed — Emergency admission

history: 38-year-old male with recurrent right flank pain for 3 days. USG shows 8mm calculus at right vesicoureteric junction with grade 2 hydronephrosis. Serum creatinine 1.4 (mildly elevated).
red_flags: Creatinine 1.4 — obstructive uropathy affecting renal function
progression: Two previous stone episodes. Current stone too large for medical expulsive therapy. Ureterorenoscopy planned.

clinical_timeline.event: Admission, IV analgesia, urine culture sent
Pre-op workup completed, urine sterile
Ureteroscopy + laser lithotripsy performed, DJ stent placed
Discharge with stent, follow-up in 4 weeks for stent removal

best_treatment: Ureteroscopic laser lithotripsy + DJ stent placement
secondary_treatment: Metabolic stone workup, increased hydration, potassium citrate if hypocitraturic
risk_reasoning: Obstructive uropathy with mild renal impairment requires timely intervention but is not immediately life-threatening. Standard urological procedure with good prognosis.
ai_rationale: 8mm obstructing stone with renal function impact requires elective URS. Risk score 5 reflects non-emergency but functionally significant obstruction. Cash patient — straightforward surgical pathway.

next_action.action: Follow up for elective admission — surgical conversion opportunity

history: 51-year-old female with known alcoholic liver cirrhosis presenting with 3 episodes of haematemesis. BP 90/60, HR 118. Haemoglobin 7.2 g/dL. Jaundiced.
progression: Acute onset haematemesis at home. Third episode in 6 months. Not on beta-blocker prophylaxis.

clinical_timeline.event: ER resuscitation, 2 units PRBC transfusion, IV Terlipressin started
Urgent OGD — 3 columns of Grade 3 oesophageal varices, band ligation x4
Bleeding controlled, IV PPI + antibiotics (SBP prophylaxis)
Child-Pugh score assessed, MELD calculated for transplant listing
Discharge on Carvedilol, PPI, lactulose with hepatology follow-up

best_treatment: Endoscopic variceal band ligation, Terlipressin infusion, prophylactic antibiotics, PPI
secondary_treatment: Non-selective beta-blocker prophylaxis, hepatology review, alcohol cessation, transplant evaluation
risk_reasoning: Active variceal haemorrhage with haemodynamic compromise. Cirrhosis Child-Pugh B with 15-20% 30-day rebleed mortality. Requires immediate endoscopic intervention and ICU-level care.
ai_rationale: Variceal bleed with shock requires emergency endoscopy within 12 hours. High rebleed risk without secondary prophylaxis. Alcohol history suggests compliance challenges.

next_action.action: Admit to ICU/CCU immediately

history: 68-year-old male, 40 pack-year smoker, with increasing breathlessness and productive cough for 5 days. SpO2 84% on room air, pCO2 58 on ABG. On home oxygen.
progression: Bacterial exacerbation triggered by upper respiratory infection. Two previous hospital admissions this year.

clinical_timeline.event: ER admission, controlled O2 therapy, NIV (BiPAP) started, antibiotics
ABG improved: pH 7.36, pCO2 50 on BiPAP
IV Methylprednisolone + Salbutamol + Ipratropium nebulization
Off BiPAP, maintaining SpO2 90% on 2L O2
Discharge with home BiPAP, rescue antibiotics, pulm rehab referral

best_treatment: NIV (BiPAP), controlled oxygen therapy, IV Methylprednisolone, antibiotics (Amoxicillin-Clavulanate)
secondary_treatment: Smoking cessation, pulmonary rehabilitation, long-acting bronchodilators optimization
risk_reasoning: Hypercapnic respiratory failure with pH 7.30 requiring NIV. GOLD Stage III COPD with recurrent admissions. High risk of intubation if BiPAP fails.
ai_rationale: Severe AECOPD with type 2 respiratory failure — BiPAP threshold criteria met. Recurrent admission pattern indicates inadequate outpatient control. Insurance enables full respiratory support pathway.

next_action.action: Arrange Respiratory HDU bed — Emergency admission

history: 58-year-old male with CKD Stage 4 (baseline creatinine 4.2) presenting with creatinine 7.8, potassium 6.8 mEq/L, oliguria for 3 days. Known diabetic nephropathy.
progression: Progressive worsening over 3 days after NSAID use for joint pain. Now with ECG changes (peaked T waves).

clinical_timeline.event: ER admission, IV Calcium gluconate, insulin-dextrose for hyperkalaemia
Emergency haemodialysis session x4 hours
Potassium corrected to 5.2, creatinine 6.1
Second HD session, AV fistula assessment
Stable for discharge, planned MWF haemodialysis schedule
Discharged with outpatient dialysis unit enrollment

best_treatment: Emergency haemodialysis, hyperkalaemia management, fluid removal
secondary_treatment: AV fistula creation planning, renal diet counseling, avoid nephrotoxins, transplant workup
risk_reasoning: Life-threatening hyperkalaemia (K+ 6.8) with ECG changes. Dialysis-dependent AKI-on-CKD. Arrhythmia and cardiac arrest risk without immediate intervention.
ai_rationale: Potassium 6.8 with ECG changes is a medical emergency. CKD Stage 4 with acute decompensation likely requires permanent dialysis. High revenue from ongoing dialysis dependency.

next_action.action: Admit to ICU/CCU immediately

history: 24-year-old male with 36-hour history of central abdominal pain migrating to right iliac fossa, fever 38.8°C, vomiting. Guarding and rigidity on examination. Alvarado score 9.
progression: Delayed presentation by 36 hours. CT abdomen confirms perforated appendicitis with peri-appendicular abscess.

clinical_timeline.event: ER admission, IV antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam) started
Emergency laparoscopic appendicectomy + peritoneal washout
Post-op recovery, IV antibiotics continued
Tolerating oral, apyrexial, wound healthy
Discharge with oral antibiotics for 5 days

best_treatment: Emergency laparoscopic appendicectomy + peritoneal washout, IV antibiotics
secondary_treatment: Post-op IV to oral antibiotic step-down, early mobilization, wound care
risk_reasoning: Perforated appendicitis with peritonitis. Delay increases sepsis risk. Young otherwise healthy patient — good surgical candidate with expected full recovery.
ai_rationale: Perforation confirmed on CT. Alvarado 9 mandates surgical intervention. Young healthy patient reduces anaesthetic risk. Cash patient — upfront payment likely.

next_action.action: Arrange Surgical Ward bed — Emergency admission

primary_diagnosis: Systemic Lupus Erythematosus — Lupus Nephritis Class III
history: 34-year-old female with known SLE presenting with worsening facial rash, joint pains, proteinuria 3.5g/day, creatinine 1.8 (rising). On Hydroxychloroquine.
progression: Progressive renal deterioration over 6 weeks. Anti-dsDNA titre elevated 4x baseline.

clinical_timeline.event: Admission, renal biopsy performed — Class III LN confirmed
IV Methylprednisolone pulse therapy x3 days
Cyclophosphamide induction initiated
Proteinuria reducing, creatinine stable at 1.6
Discharge on oral prednisolone taper + Mycophenolate, tight BP control

best_treatment: IV Methylprednisolone pulse x3 days, Cyclophosphamide induction (NIH protocol)
secondary_treatment: Mycophenolate maintenance, Hydroxychloroquine continuation, BP control, anticoagulation for APS
risk_reasoning: Lupus nephritis Class III with nephrotic-range proteinuria and rising creatinine. APS increases thrombosis risk during immunosuppression. Risk of progression to Class IV if undertreated.
ai_rationale: Biopsy-confirmed Class III LN requires induction immunosuppression. APS co-morbidity adds complexity. Insurance enables full biopsy + pulse steroid pathway.

next_action.action: Schedule admission within 24–48 hours — Rheumatology / Nephrology Ward

history: 52-year-old male with Non-Hodgkin's Lymphoma on R-CHOP chemotherapy, Day 10 post-cycle 4, presenting with fever 39.2°C, ANC 0.2 x10^9/L.
progression: Day 10 post-R-CHOP chemotherapy — nadir period. Fever developed acutely. Blood cultures drawn.

clinical_timeline.event: Emergency admission, broad-spectrum IV antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam + Vancomycin)
Blood cultures: E. coli bacteraemia — antibiotics tailored
Afebrile, ANC recovering to 0.8
G-CSF given, ANC 2.1, oral antibiotics to complete 14-day course
Discharge with oncology follow-up, next chemo cycle delay discussed

best_treatment: IV broad-spectrum antibiotics (Piperacillin-Tazobactam), targeted therapy on culture results, G-CSF
secondary_treatment: Antifungal prophylaxis if not defervesced by 72h, next chemo cycle timing reassessment
deferred_time: Next chemo cycle deferred 1-2 weeks pending ANC recovery
risk_reasoning: High-risk febrile neutropenia (MASCC 15) with confirmed bacteraemia. ANC 0.2 with no immune reserve. Sepsis risk without urgent antibiotic coverage.
ai_rationale: MASCC score 15 mandates inpatient IV antibiotics. E. coli bacteraemia confirms septic source. G-CSF to shorten neutropenia duration. Insurance covers full oncological support.

next_action.action: Admit to ICU/CCU immediately

history: 22-year-old female, brought by family with 3-week history of auditory hallucinations, paranoid delusions, social withdrawal, stopped eating. No prior psychiatric history. No substance use.
red_flags: Not eating for 5 days — nutritional compromise
Risk of self-harm during psychotic episode
progression: Insidious onset 3 weeks ago. Acutely agitated today, refusing to communicate.

clinical_timeline.event: Emergency admission, PANSS assessment, medical workup to rule organic cause
CT brain, thyroid, metabolic screen — all normal
Antipsychotic initiated — Risperidone 2mg, titrated slowly
Agitation settling, eating small amounts
Significant improvement in delusions, hallucinations reduced
Discharge on Risperidone 4mg, CMHC follow-up, family psychoeducation done

best_treatment: Atypical antipsychotic (Risperidone), structured psychiatric ward environment, psychoeducation
secondary_treatment: Family therapy, cognitive behavioural therapy for psychosis (CBTp), social work assessment
risk_reasoning: First episode psychosis with nutritional compromise and self-harm risk. Early intervention critical to long-term prognosis. Requires inpatient stabilization.
ai_rationale: First episode psychosis requiring inpatient initiation of antipsychotics. Nutritional risk adds medical complexity. Cash patient — family likely to be involved in care decisions.
icd10_verification_note: Requires medical coder review before billing

next_action.action: Schedule admission within 24–48 hours — Psychiatric Ward

primary_diagnosis: Central Retinal Artery Occlusion Right Eye
history: 67-year-old male with sudden painless loss of vision in right eye for 2 hours. Known hypertensive, atrial fibrillation on aspirin. Fundoscopy: cherry red spot, pallid retina.
red_flags: Possible central embolic event — stroke risk
progression: Acute onset 2 hours ago. Vision 6/60 right eye. Possible embolic source from AF.

clinical_timeline.event: Emergency ophthalmology assessment, IOP-lowering measures + ocular massage
Urgent neurology referral — TIA/stroke workup initiated
MRI brain: small cortical infarct confirmed — embolic source
Anticoagulation started (NOAC), carotid doppler arranged
Vision partially improved to 6/24, referred to low vision rehabilitation

best_treatment: Emergency IOP reduction, inhalation of carbogen, ocular massage, anticoagulation for embolic source
secondary_treatment: Stroke prevention — NOAC, hypertension control, statin, low vision rehab
risk_reasoning: CRAO in time-critical window with concurrent embolic stroke. AF untreated is ongoing stroke risk. Simultaneous ophthalmological and neurological emergency.
ai_rationale: CRAO with concurrent TIA/stroke requires dual-specialty emergency management. AF with embolic source needs anticoagulation urgently. Insurance enables full workup.

next_action.action: Arrange Ophthalmology / Neurology Ward bed — Emergency admission

history: 45-year-old female with known pemphigus vulgaris presenting with extensive blistering involving >30% BSA, painful oral mucosa lesions, difficulty swallowing. On low-dose prednisolone.
red_flags: Secondary infection risk in open lesions
progression: Breakthrough flare despite maintenance prednisolone. Blisters spreading rapidly over 5 days.

clinical_timeline.event: Admission, wound care, IV Methylprednisolone pulse initiated
Rituximab infusion scheduled — steroid-sparing approach
Nutritional support via NGT for dysphagia
New blister formation reduced, mucosa healing
Oral intake resumed, Rituximab second dose given
Discharge on prednisolone taper + dermatology follow-up

best_treatment: IV Methylprednisolone pulse, Rituximab infusion, wound care, nutritional support
secondary_treatment: Mycophenolate mofetil as steroid-sparing agent, bone protection for osteoporosis
risk_reasoning: Extensive pemphigus flare with >30% BSA and mucosal involvement. Fluid and electrolyte losses, secondary infection, and nutritional compromise are key risks.
ai_rationale: Extensive PV flare requiring immunosuppression escalation with Rituximab. BSA >30% necessitates inpatient wound care and nutritional support. Cash patient — high out-of-pocket cost.
icd10_verification_note: Requires medical coder review before billing

next_action.action: Schedule admission within 24–48 hours — Dermatology Ward / Burns-type Care

inferred_procedure_name: Deep Neck Space Infection — Surgical Drainage
history: 35-year-old male with 5-day history of worsening sore throat, trismus, high fever 40°C, drooling. Neck swelling right side. CT confirms parapharyngeal collection 3.5cm.
progression: Started as dental infection 10 days ago, now evolved to deep neck space abscess. Airway narrowing on CT.

clinical_timeline.event: Emergency admission, anesthesia on standby, IV antibiotics (Ampicillin-Sulbactam + Metronidazole)
Emergency surgical drainage under GA, 30mL pus drained
Airway stable, trismus improving
Drain output decreasing, tolerating soft diet
Afebrile, drain removed, IV to oral antibiotics
Discharge with dental referral, diabetic management review

best_treatment: Emergency surgical drainage, IV broad-spectrum antibiotics, glycaemic control
secondary_treatment: Dental source treatment, diabetic optimization, outpatient IV-to-oral antibiotics
risk_reasoning: Parapharyngeal abscess with trismus and airway narrowing — risk of complete airway obstruction and descending mediastinitis. Diabetes impairs immune response and healing.
ai_rationale: Airway compromise risk mandates emergency drainage. Diabetic patient with deep neck infection has mediastinitis risk requiring aggressive surgical and antibiotic management.

next_action.action: Arrange ENT Ward / ICU Backup bed — Emergency admission

primary_diagnosis: Sickle Cell Disease — Acute Vaso-occlusive Crisis
history: 29-year-old female with known sickle cell disease presenting with severe bilateral leg pain, pain score 9/10, fever 38.5°C. Haemoglobin 6.8 g/dL. 4th crisis this year.
progression: Triggered by dehydration and cold exposure. Escalating pain despite oral analgesia at home.

clinical_timeline.event: IV fluids, IV morphine PCA, supplemental oxygen, folic acid
Blood transfusion: 2 units PRBC — Hb improved to 8.5
Chest X-ray clear — ACS ruled out
Pain improving, PCA weaned to oral analgesia
Discharge with hydroxyurea dose review, haematology follow-up

best_treatment: IV fluids, IV morphine PCA, blood transfusion if Hb <7, supplemental oxygen
secondary_treatment: Hydroxyurea dose optimization, folic acid, avoidance triggers, bone marrow transplant evaluation
risk_reasoning: Frequent vaso-occlusive crises (4th this year) with anaemia and pulmonary hypertension. Acute chest syndrome risk during crisis. Poorly controlled disease requiring management escalation.
ai_rationale: Recurrent crises indicate suboptimal hydroxyurea dosing. Pulmonary hypertension co-morbidity elevates ACS risk. Insurance enables full transfusion pathway and specialist review.

next_action.action: Arrange Haematology Ward bed — Emergency admission

history: 32-year-old male, agricultural worker, presenting with 8-day fever, headache, eschar on right axilla, hepatosplenomegaly, platelet 45,000. Weil-Felix positive.
progression: 8 days of fever without diagnosis in rural setting. Eschar identified on examination — classic scrub typhus sign.

clinical_timeline.event: Admission, Doxycycline 100mg BD started, LFT and platelet monitoring
Fever reducing, Weil-Felix titre 1:80 — diagnosis confirmed
Platelet rising to 78,000, LFT improving
Afebrile, tolerating oral, Doxycycline 14-day course
Discharge with complete oral course, advised re-check in 1 week

best_treatment: Doxycycline 100mg BD for 14 days, supportive care, platelet monitoring
secondary_treatment: Vector control counseling, protective clothing education, liver support
risk_reasoning: Complicated scrub typhus with multi-organ involvement (liver, spleen, platelets) but no CNS involvement yet. Treatable with early Doxycycline — risk of encephalitis if delayed.
ai_rationale: Classic scrub typhus with eschar and thrombocytopenia. Highly responsive to Doxycycline. Cash patient from agricultural background — cost-conscious, ensure affordable discharge regimen.
icd10_verification_note: Requires medical coder review before billing

next_action.action: Schedule follow-up appointment and monitor progress

history: 78-year-old female with 3 falls in last 2 months, on 11 medications including 3 antihypertensives, 2 psychotropics. Postural hypotension documented. Frailty score 4/7.
red_flags: 3 falls in 2 months — high fracture risk
progression: Increasing fall frequency. Last fall caused bruising. Medication complexity preventing adequate BP control and contributing to postural hypotension.

clinical_timeline.event: Comprehensive geriatric assessment, MMSE, timed-up-and-go test
Medication review — deprescribed 4 medications including 1 psychotropic
Physiotherapy fall risk assessment, home modification advised
Discharge with simplified medication regimen, OT referral, vitamin D started

best_treatment: Medication deprescribing, fall prevention physiotherapy, home safety assessment
secondary_treatment: Vitamin D + calcium supplementation, bone density scan, carer education
risk_reasoning: Frail elderly with polypharmacy-driven falls. Not acutely life-threatening but high fracture risk with next fall. Elective admission enables safe deprescribing and fall prevention planning.
ai_rationale: Polypharmacy review in frail elderly prevents high-cost fracture admissions. Risk score 5 reflects cumulative vulnerability rather than acute crisis. Elective admission yields high preventive value.
icd10_verification_note: Requires medical coder review before billing

next_action.action: Follow up for elective admission — surgical conversion opportunity
